# n8n MCP vs Claude Code: The 2025 Automation Showdown

Should you build automations with n8n's new MCP integration or code them from scratch using Claude Code? Here's the decision framework for 2025.

K

[Kevin Michael Schindler](https://evalics.com/author/kevin-michael-schindler)

February 24, 2026·9 min read

![n8n MCP vs Claude Code: The 2025 Automation Showdown](https://evalics.com/blogs/top/n8n-mcp-vs-claude-code-automation.png)

### Table of Contents

You have a complex business process to automate. In 2024, the choice was usually between a visual builder like Zapier/Make or hiring a developer.

In 2025, the game has changed completely.

Now, you have two AI-driven superpowers fighting for dominance:

1. **n8n with MCP (Model Context Protocol):** An AI agent (inside Cursor or Claude Desktop) that "drives" the n8n visual builder for you.
2. **Claude Code:** An autonomous CLI agent that writes, tests, and deploys raw code (Python/Node.js) directly from your terminal.

The line between "low-code" and "pro-code" hasn't just blurred; it’s vanished. But choosing the wrong path can still cost you weeks of maintenance headaches.

Here is the definitive guide to choosing between the **Architect Approach (n8n MCP)** and the **Builder Approach (Claude Code)**.

## The Contenders Defined

Before we pick a winner, let's clarify what these tools actually do in a modern workflow.

### 1\. n8n + MCP (The AI Architect)

n8n has adopted the **Model Context Protocol (MCP)**. Think of this as giving your AI "hands." Instead of you dragging and dropping nodes, you connect Cursor or Claude Desktop to your n8n instance.

You say: _"Build a workflow that takes new Stripe payments and puts them into Airtable."_

The AI talks to n8n, adds the nodes, configures the connections, and hands you a finished visual workflow. You get the observability of a visual tool without the tedious clicking.

### 2\. Claude Code (The AI Builder)

Claude Code is Anthropic’s autonomous coding agent. It lives in your terminal. It doesn't use a drag-and-drop interface. It writes raw code files (usually Python or TypeScript).

You say: _"Write a script that monitors Stripe for payments and updates Airtable."_

It writes the code, installs the libraries (`pip install airtable-python-wrapper`), runs the tests, fixes its own bugs, and deploys the script.

> **Key Insight:** n8n MCP is about **orchestrating pre-built blocks**. Claude Code is about **manufacturing custom logic**.

## Round 1: Handling Authentication (The "OAuth Nightmare")

The biggest friction point in automation isn't logic; it's permission.

**The n8n Advantage:**
n8n shines here. It has thousands of pre-built nodes with authentication handled for you. You don't need to read the Gmail API documentation to learn how to refresh a token. You just click "Sign in with Google." Even when using MCP, the AI utilizes these pre-existing connections.

**The Claude Code Reality:**
If you ask Claude Code to "read my emails," it has to write code to handle the Google Cloud Console OAuth flow. It needs to store tokens securely. It needs to handle token refreshes.

**Winner:** **n8n**. If your automation relies on connecting SaaS apps (Slack, Gmail, HubSpot, Salesforce), n8n saves you hours of "token management" hell.

## Round 2: Data Processing & Logic

This is where the visual paradigm often breaks down.

Imagine you need to:

1. Scrape 500 websites.
2. Extract specific pricing data using Regex.
3. Compare it to a CSV file with 50,000 rows.
4. Formatting the output as a PDF.

**The n8n Struggle:**
Doing this in n8n (or Make) requires loops. Visual loops are notoriously slow and consume a massive amount of "execution" credits. Managing complex Regex in a small text box inside a node is frustrating.

**The Claude Code Advantage:**
Claude Code will write a Python script using `BeautifulSoup` and `Pandas`. It processes 50,000 rows in seconds, not minutes. It handles complex logic naturally because code is _meant_ for logic. It doesn't care if the logic has 50 branches; a visual builder becomes a "spaghetti monster" with that many routes.

![Bar chart comparing processing speed: 14 minutes for n8n loops vs 22 seconds for Python script](https://evalics.com/blogs/n8n_mcp_vs/bar-chart-comparing-processing-speed-14-minutes-fo.png)

**Winner:** **Claude Code**. For heavy data lifting, scraping, or complex algorithmic logic, raw code is superior.

## Round 3: Maintenance & Observability

You built the automation. It runs for a month. Then it breaks. Who fixes it?

**The "Bus Factor" with Claude Code:**
If you built a Python script using Claude Code, but you _don't know Python_, you are in a dangerous spot. If the script fails, you have to spin up the agent again and hope it can diagnose the error from the logs. You cannot simply "look" at the code and see where the data stopped flowing unless you are a developer.

**The n8n Safety Net:**
n8n provides visual execution history. You can see the green line turn red. You can click the exact node that failed and see the input/output JSON. Even non-technical team members can look at an n8n workflow and understand _roughly_ what it's supposed to do.

> **Pro Tip:** If you are handing this automation off to a client or a non-technical operations manager, **use n8n**. Handing them a Python script they can't read is a recipe for churn.

## Comparison: The Decision Matrix

Here is how the two approaches stack up for specific business needs.

| Feature | n8n (via MCP/Cursor) | Claude Code (Raw Code) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Setup Speed** | Very High (Pre-built nodes) | High (Fast generation) |
| **Connectivity** | Excellent (Managed Auth) | Good (Requires API setup) |
| **Scalability** | Medium (Node execution limits) | High (Server/Lambda limits only) |
| **Debugging** | Visual / Intuitive | Requires coding knowledge |
| **Hosting** | Self-hosted or Cloud | AWS Lambda, Vercel, VPS |
| **Best For** | SaaS Interconnectivity | Data Processing / Scraping |

## How Cursor Fits Into the n8n Workflow

You might be wondering: _"Why use Cursor with n8n? Isn't n8n drag-and-drop?"_

This is the power of the **n8n MCP integration**.

Traditionally, n8n requires you to know JavaScript for the "Code Node" if you want to do something custom. Now, inside Cursor (the editor), you can have your n8n workflow open on one side and the AI chat on the other.

1. **Connect Cursor to n8n:** You provide Cursor with your n8n API key via MCP.
2. **Context Awareness:** Cursor can "read" your current workflow JSON.
3. **Complex Transformations:** You can ask Cursor: _"Look at the JSON output from the Shopify node. Write a JavaScript function in the Code Node to filter out orders under $50 and format the rest for Slack."_

Cursor writes the code _directly into the n8n node_. You get the best of both worlds: the visual structure of n8n and the coding power of an LLM.

![Cursor editor controlling n8n workflow via MCP integration](https://evalics.com/blogs/n8n_mcp_vs/cursor-editor-controlling-n8n-workflow-via-mcp-int.png)

## The Hybrid Approach: The "Function Calling" Model

The smartest engineers in 2025 aren't choosing just one. They are combining them.

We call this the **Core & Satellite** method.

- **The Core (n8n):** Use n8n as the backbone. It handles the triggers (Webhooks, Cron jobs), the authentication (connecting to Gmail/Slack), and the routing.
- **The Satellite (Code):** When you hit a complex data step, don't try to solve it with 20 n8n nodes. Use an "HTTP Request" node or a "Code Node."
- **The Execution:** Use Claude Code or Cursor to write a robust Python/JS script that does the heavy lifting. Host that script as a serverless function (or just paste it into the n8n Code Node).

> **Reality Check:** Don't be a purist. Pure code is hard to maintain for simple things (like sending a Slack DM). Pure no-code is hard to maintain for complex things (like fuzzy matching strings). Mix them.

## 3 Rules for Choosing Your Path

If you are staring at a blank screen right now, here is your decision logic:

1. **Is the main difficulty "Authentication"?**(e.g., I need to listen to Outlook emails and save attachments to OneDrive).

   - **Choose n8n.** The OAuth handling alone is worth the price of admission.
2. **Is the main difficulty "Logic/Looping"?**(e.g., I need to cross-reference a 20MB CSV against a SQL database and fuzzy match names).

   - **Choose Claude Code.** Build a standalone script. n8n will time out or cost a fortune in execution credits.
3. **Do you need a UI?**(e.g., I need a form for a human to approve the draft).

   - **Choose n8n.** It has built-in "Wait for Form" nodes and approval logic. Building a UI from scratch with code is overkill for internal tools.

## Conclusion

The debate between "n8n MCP" and "Claude Code" isn't really about which tool is better—it's about where you want the complexity to live.

With **n8n MCP**, the complexity lives in the **graph**. You see lines and nodes. It is visual, accessible, and easier to hand off.

With **Claude Code**, the complexity lives in the **syntax**. It is faster, cheaper to run at scale, and infinitely flexible, but opaque to non-coders.

**My recommendation for 2025:**
Start with **n8n**. Use the new MCP capabilities in Cursor to write complex transformations inside the Code Nodes. Only graduate to a pure Claude Code/Python build if you hit a performance wall or a cost ceiling.

Automation is about results, not code purity. Pick the tool that lets you sleep at night knowing the bot is working.

**Ready to modernize your automation stack?**
Don't let legacy workflows slow you down. [Book a demo with Evalics](https://calendly.com/kevin-schindler/30min?back=1&month=2025-10) to see how we blend n8n and AI agents for enterprise-grade efficiency.

## Frequently Asked Questions

What is n8n MCP?

n8n MCP (Model Context Protocol) allows AI agents like Claude or Cursor to directly connect to your n8n instance. This lets the AI read your workflows, edit nodes, and trigger automations without you needing to manually click through the UI.

Is Claude Code better than n8n for automation?

It depends on the complexity. Claude Code is better for heavy data processing, scraping, and complex logic where visual builders become slow. n8n is better for 'glue' tasks connecting standard apps (like Slack to Gmail) because it handles authentication for you.

Can Cursor use n8n MCP?

Yes. Since Cursor supports the Model Context Protocol, you can add your n8n instance as an MCP server. This allows you to ask Cursor to 'fix the error in my n8n workflow' directly from your editor.

[Skip to content](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/#accessing-and-using-n8n-mcp-server) [Edit this page](https://github.com/n8n-io/n8n-docs/edit/main/docs/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server.md "Edit this page") # Accessing and using n8n MCP server [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#accessing-and-using-n8n-mcp-server "Permanent link") Connect supported MCP clients to your n8n workflows through n8n's built-in MCP server. The server allows clients such as Lovable or Claude Desktop to connect securely to an n8n instance. Once connected, these clients can: - Search for your workflows - Interact with workflows marked as available in MCP - Trigger and test exposed workflows - Create and edit workflows and data tables ## Difference between instance-level MCP access and MCP Server Trigger node [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#difference-between-instance-level-mcp-access-and-mcp-server-trigger-node "Permanent link") Instance-level MCP access lets you create one connection per n8n instance, use centralized authentication, and choose which workflows to enable for access. Enabled workflows are easy to find and run without extra setup for each workflow. In comparison, you configure an MCP Server Trigger node inside a single workflow. This node exposes tools only from that workflow, a useful approach when you want to craft a specific MCP server behavior within one workflow. ### Key considerations when using instance-level MCP access [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#key-considerations-when-using-instance-level-mcp-access "Permanent link") - MCP supports two types of workflow interactions: running existing workflows with the workflow execution tools, and building or editing workflows (v2.13 onward). - It doesn’t provide blanket exposure to all workflows in your instance. You must enable MCP at the instance level and then enable each workflow individually. The only exception here is the `search_workflows` tool, which is able to access all workflows current user has access to but it will only be able to surface previews, not the full workflow data. - It's not scoped to each MCP client. All clients you connect (for example, Claude Desktop and ChatGPT) can see all workflows you've enabled for MCP access. You can't restrict specific workflows to specific clients. On a user level, visibility remains user-scoped: users can only see MCP-enabled workflows they have access to. - Most MCP tools work on unpublished workflows. The exception is `execute_workflow`, which defaults to production mode and runs the published version of a workflow. It also supports a `manual` execution mode to run the current (unpublished) version. ## Enabling MCP access [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#enabling-mcp-access "Permanent link") ### For Cloud and self-hosted instances [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#for-cloud-and-self-hosted-instances "Permanent link") 1. Navigate to **Settings > Instance-level MCP** 2. Toggle **Enable MCP access** (requires instance owner or admin permissions). ![enable-mcp-access.png](https://docs.n8n.io/_images/advanced-ai/enable-mcp-access.png) Once enabled, you'll see: 1. List of workflows exposed to MCP clients 2. List of connected OAuth clients 3. Main MCP toggle to enable/disable instance-level access 4. _Connection details_ button that shows detailed instructions for connecting MCP clients ![mcp_page_content.png](https://docs.n8n.io/_images/advanced-ai/mcp_page_content.png) **To disable:** Toggle the main MCP toggle off. Environment variables (self-hosted only) On self-hosted instances, you can also manage MCP settings using environment variables. See [Manage instance settings using environment variables](https://docs.n8n.io/hosting/configuration/settings-env-vars/#mcp). ### For self-hosted: Complete disablement [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#for-self-hosted-complete-disablement "Permanent link") To remove the feature entirely, set the environment variable: `N8N_DISABLED_MODULES=mcp` This action removes MCP endpoints and hides all related UI elements. ## Setting up MCP authentication [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#setting-up-mcp-authentication "Permanent link") The **Connection details** popup menu provides two authentication options for MCP clients: - **OAuth2** - **Access Token** ![mcp_connect_menu.png](https://docs.n8n.io/_images/advanced-ai/mcp_connect_menu.png) ### Using OAuth2 [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#using-oauth2 "Permanent link") Copy your instance server URL from the **OAuth** tab and use it to configure your MCP client. After connecting, the client will redirect you to n8n to authorize access. #### Revoking client access [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#revoking-client-access "Permanent link") To revoke access for connected MCP clients: 1. Navigate to **Settings > Instance-level MCP**. 2. Switch to the **Connected clients** tab. You should see a table of connected OAuth clients. 3. Use the action menu in each client's row to revoke access for specific clients. ![mcp_revoke_client_access.png](https://docs.n8n.io/_images/advanced-ai/mcp_revoke_client_access.png) ### Using Access Token [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#using-access-token "Permanent link") Use your instance server URL and your personal MCP Access Token from the **Access Token** tab on the _Connection details_ menu. When you first visit the **MCP Access page**, n8n automatically generates a personal MCP Access Token tied to your user account. Info Copy your token right away. On future visits, you'll only see a redacted value and the copy button will be disabled. #### Rotating your token [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#rotating-your-token "Permanent link") If you lose your token or need to rotate it: 1. Navigate to **Settings > Instance-level MCP**. 2. Open the _Connection details_ menu by clicking the button in the top-right corner. 3. Switch to the **Access Token** tab. 4. Generate a new token using the button next to the redacted token value. n8n revokes the previous token when you generate a new one. 5. Update all connected MCP clients with the new value. ![mcp_rotate_token.png](https://docs.n8n.io/_images/advanced-ai/mcp_rotate_token.png) ## Exposing workflows to MCP clients [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#exposing-workflows-to-mcp-clients "Permanent link") By default, no workflows are visible to MCP clients. You must explicitly enable MCP access for each workflow you want to expose. ### Enabling access [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#enabling-access "Permanent link") #### Option 1: From MCP settings page (available from n8n v2.2.0) [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#option-1-from-mcp-settings-page-available-from-n8n-v220 "Permanent link") 1. Click the **Enable workflows** button (in the workflows table header or in the table's empty state) 2. Search for the desired workflow (by name or description) and select it from the list 3. Click **Enable** button to confirm #### Option 2: From the workflow editor [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#option-2-from-the-workflow-editor "Permanent link") 1. Open the workflow. 2. Click the main workflow menu (`...`) in the top-right corner. 3. Select **Settings**. 4. Toggle **Available in MCP**. #### Option 3: From the workflows list [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#option-3-from-the-workflows-list "Permanent link") 1. Go to **Workflows**. 2. Open the menu on a workflow card. 3. Select **Enable MCP access**. ### Managing access [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#managing-access "Permanent link") The **Instance-level MCP** settings page shows all workflows available to MCP clients. From this list you can: - Open a workflow, its home project or parent folder directly - Revoke access using the action menu (or use **Disable MCP access** from the workflow card menu) - Update workflow description using the action menu (or use the menu in the workflow editor) - Enable access for more workflows using the **Enable workflows** button (available from n8n v2.2.0) ### Workflow descriptions [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#workflow-descriptions "Permanent link") To help MCP clients identify workflows, you can add free-text descriptions as follows: 1. Option 1: From the **Instance-level MCP** page 1. Navigate to **Settings > Instance-level MCP**. 2. Make sure you are on the **Workflows** tab. 3. Use the action menu in the desired workflow's row and select the **Edit description** action. 4. Alternatively, click the description text directly to open the edit dialog. 2. Option 2: From the workflow editor 1. Open the workflow. 2. Click the main workflow menu (`...`) in the top-right corner. 3. Select **Edit description**. ![mcp_workflow_description.png](https://docs.n8n.io/_images/advanced-ai/mcp_workflow_description.png) ## Tools and resources [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#tools-and-resources "Permanent link") Tip Consider using coding agents (such as Claude Code or Google ADK agents) instead of chat clients as your MCP clients. Coding agents are optimized for generating and validating TypeScript code, making them ideal for building workflows programmatically. The n8n MCP server exposes tools for workflow management, workflow building, and data tables. For a complete list of available tools and their parameters, refer to the [MCP server tools reference](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/mcp_tools_reference/). ## Examples [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#examples "Permanent link") #### Connecting Lovable to n8n MCP server [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#connecting-lovable-to-n8n-mcp-server "Permanent link") 1. Configure MCP Server in Lovable (OAuth). - Navigate to your workspace  **Settings > Integrations**. - In the **MCP Servers** section, find **n8n** and click **Connect**. - Enter your n8n server URL (shown on the **MCP Access** page). - Save the connection. If successful, n8n redirects you to authorize Lovable. 2. Verify connectivity. - Once connected, Lovable can query for workflows with MCP access enabled. - **Example:** Asking Lovable to build a workflow UI that lists users and allows deleting them. #### Connecting Claude Desktop to n8n MCP server [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#connecting-claude-desktop-to-n8n-mcp-server "Permanent link") ##### Using OAuth2 [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#using-oauth2_1 "Permanent link") 1. Navigate to **Settings** \> **Connectors** in Claude Desktop. 2. Click on **Add custom connector**. 3. Enter the following details: - **Name:** n8n MCP - **Remote MCP Server URL**: Your n8n base URL (shown on the **Instance-level MCP** page) 4. Save the connector. 5. When prompted, authorize Claude Desktop to access your n8n instance. ##### Using Access Token [\#](https://docs.n8n.io/advanced-ai/mcp/accessing-n8n-mcp-server/\#using-access-token_1 "Permanent link") Add the following entry to your `claude_desktop_config.json` file: | | | | --- | --- | | ```

![Giving Claude Code the Keys to n8n: MCP for Workflow Automation, Part 4 of the Series](https://media.licdn.com/dms/image/v2/D5612AQH7iDQQtB_-IA/article-cover_image-shrink_720_1280/B56Zru3A1vMIAI-/0/1764943994735?e=2147483647&v=beta&t=90Uk0fiV8mTYipQsHpouYDdAi0ySYkVJQYmaZ8vhcVs)

William LawyerVP of Information Systems with 23 years experience managing, implementing and supporting IT systems

* * *

In [Part 1](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/williamrlawyer_after-23-years-in-traditional-it-and-flirting-activity-7397298239982505984-wdI3?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAG8ofwB_Vfb6Lu4VODNEEVP6EfGReJ3ACg&trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse_little-text-block), I built a local AI server. In [Part 2](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/williamrlawyer_ai-developerexperience-automation-activity-7397656380830232576-gog1?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAG8ofwB_Vfb6Lu4VODNEEVP6EfGReJ3ACg&trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse_little-text-block), I connected Claude Code to Proxmox so it could spin up VMs on demand. In [Part 3](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/williamrlawyer_ai-claudecode-gemini3-activity-7401304886023483392-l5GG?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAG8ofwB_Vfb6Lu4VODNEEVP6EfGReJ3ACg&trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse_little-text-block), I started orchestrating multiple AI agents, local and frontier models, working together under Claude Code's coordination.

Each step extended what my AI agent could control. But there was still a gap.

I'd be deep in a Claude Code session, building application logic, and then hit the point where I needed an automation workflow watch this inbox, process these documents, notify this system. That meant switching to a browser, opening n8n's visual editor, manually wiring nodes together, testing, failing, iterating. Every context switch broke my flow.

So I built an MCP server for n8n. Now Claude Code can create, modify, execute, and debug automation workflows through conversation. No more switching tools. No more manual node wiring. The entire automation layer is now under AI agent control.

This is Part 4.

* * *

What is n8n?

For those unfamiliar: n8n is an open-source workflow automation platform. Think of it as the connective tissue between all your business systems. It lets you build automated workflows that respond to triggers, process data, and take actions across hundreds of integrations.

A typical n8n workflow might:

- Watch an email inbox for incoming invoices
- Extract data from attachments using AI
- Create records in your accounting system
- Notify the appropriate team member via Slack
- Use AI agents, easily switching between models

The key differentiator from commercial alternatives like Zapier or Make: n8n is self-hostable. You can run it on your own infrastructure, keep your data local, and avoid per-execution pricing that scales unpredictably.

* * *

The Last Mile Problem

Code is only part of what I build. For MSP and SMB work, the real value is often in integrations and automations. Connecting systems, processing data, triggering actions based on business events. n8n handles that layer.

But until now, building workflows meant constant context-switching.

I'd be in Claude Code solving a problem: "Build a document processing pipeline that extracts vendor, date, and line items from uploaded invoices." Claude would help me write the extraction logic, maybe suggest a data schema, even generate API endpoints. Great.

Then I'd need to wire it all together: watch a folder for new files, trigger the processing, route the results, handle errors, notify on completion. That meant opening n8n in a browser, manually dragging nodes onto a canvas, configuring each one, connecting them, testing, watching it fail, debugging, iterating.

Every time I switched from terminal to browser, I lost momentum. The visual node editor is powerful, but it's a completely different interface with different mental models. And debugging meant clicking through execution logs in the UI, trying to figure out what went wrong, then manually adjusting nodes.

The MCP server eliminates that friction. Now I can stay in Claude Code for the entire development cycle from application logic to automation workflow to deployment.

* * *

What the MCP Server Exposes

I built the n8n MCP server using n8n's documented REST API. Here's what Claude Code can now do:

Workflow Management

- List and search all workflows
- Retrieve a specific workflow's complete definition
- Create new workflows from JSON specifications
- Update existing workflows
- Activate or deactivate workflows
- Delete workflows

Execution Control

- Trigger workflow executions
- Retrieve execution history
- Get detailed execution data including inputs, outputs, and errors at each node
- Analyze failures and suggest fixes

Development Support

- List available node types
- Get parameter schemas for specific nodes
- Validate workflow JSON before deployment

The practical effect: I can describe what I want an automation to do, and Claude Code builds it. Or I can paste in a failing workflow, ask what's wrong, and get actionable debugging.

* * *

Running n8n Locally with Proxmox

Here's where the pieces from earlier parts start connecting.

Remember the Proxmox MCP server from Part 2? It doesn't just spin up containers it gives Claude Code direct SSH access with sudo privileges. That changes what's possible for ongoing management.

Standing up n8n is trivial. A single Proxmox LXC container running Docker, n8n installed via docker-compose. The whole process takes maybe 15 minutes manually, faster if Claude Code handles it. Self-hosted n8n means no per-execution fees, no workflow limits, and complete data sovereignty. For an MSP serving clients with sensitive data, that last point matters.

But the real value isn't just initial deployment it's ongoing access.

Claude Code can SSH directly into the container. Through the Proxmox MCP server, Claude Code has sudo access to the n8n container's filesystem. That means:

- Modify n8n configuration files directly
- Update Docker containers and restart services
- Install additional dependencies or tools
- Access n8n's local data directory for debugging
- Pull logs without opening a separate terminal
- Apply security patches and updates

When something breaks at 2am, I don't need to SSH in manually, navigate to the right directory, and start troubleshooting. I describe the problem to Claude Code: "n8n isn't starting after the last update check the Docker logs and see what's failing." Claude connects, pulls the logs, analyzes the error, and either fixes it or tells me exactly what needs to happen.

Infrastructure as conversation. Last week I needed to add a Python dependency for a custom n8n node I was building. Old approach: SSH into container, activate the right environment, pip install, verify, restart n8n. New approach: "Install pandas in the n8n container and restart the service." Done.

This extends to updates and maintenance. "Check if there's a newer n8n version available and update if the changelog looks safe." Claude Code can pull the changelog, assess whether the update is routine or risky, proceed or flag for my review. Routine maintenance becomes a conversation instead of a checklist.

The combination of container creation (Proxmox) plus direct SSH management (MCP) plus workflow control (n8n API) means the entire n8n lifecycle is under Claude Code's reach—from initial deployment through daily operation to updates and troubleshooting.

* * *

Document Processing: A Practical Example

Let me walk through something I'm actively building: an automated document processing pipeline for financial receipts and invoices.

The goal: Upload an image of a receipt, extract structured data (vendor, date, total, line items, payment method), and route it into our business systems.

Here's how development works now with the n8n MCP server:

Step 1: Describe the workflow

I tell Claude Code: "Build an n8n workflow that watches a outlook folder for new emails with or without attachments along with telegram image uploads, sends them to my local Llama vision model for extraction, parses the response into structured JSON, converts to PDF, validates the required fields are present, and posts the result to a spreadsheet with the file being stored in SharePoint. If extraction fails, move the file to an error folder and send a notification."

Step 2: Claude Code generates the workflow

Claude creates the n8n workflow JSON nodes for file watching, HTTP requests to the Ollama endpoint, JSON parsing, conditional routing, error handling, notifications. It uses the n8n MCP tools to validate the structure before deployment.

Step 3: Deploy to test environment

Using the Proxmox integration, Claude deploys the workflow. It copies my sample receipts to the watch folder and triggers execution.

Step 4: Debug and iterate

The first run fails. It always fails that's development. But now instead of clicking through n8n's execution log UI, I ask Claude: "What went wrong with that execution?"

Claude uses the MCP server to pull the execution data, analyzes the error (the Ollama response format wasn't what the JSON parser expected), and suggests a fix. It updates the workflow, redeploys, and runs again.

Step 5: Production deployment

Once the workflow passes testing, Claude deploys it to my production n8n instance and activates it. The test container gets destroyed.

Total time from concept to production workflow: about 30 minutes. Manual approach with visual editor and context-switching: 2-3 hours minimum.

A complex workflow, looks like a mess but is extremely useful for me

* * *

Debugging Workflow JSON Directly

One unexpected benefit: Claude Code can now debug workflow JSON directly.

n8n workflows are stored as JSON. They're readable, but complex nested node definitions, connection mappings, parameter configurations. When something breaks, finding the issue in raw JSON is tedious.

Now I can paste a workflow JSON into Claude Code and ask: "This workflow is failing when the invoice total exceeds $10,000. What's wrong?"

Claude can parse the JSON, understand the logic flow, identify the conditional that's incorrectly configured, and either explain the fix or generate a corrected version. It can even use the n8n MCP server to validate its fix before I deploy it.

This has been surprisingly valuable for inherited workflows. When I need to modify an automation someone else built, Claude can analyze the existing structure, explain what it does, and suggest changes all from the JSON without me needing to trace the visual representation node by node.

* * *

The Self-Hosted Stack

Step back and look at what's now possible:

- Local AI models running on my Framework Desktop (Part 1)
- On-demand infrastructure through Proxmox (Part 2)
- Multi-agent orchestration with Claude Code as coordinator (Part 3)
- Workflow automation through self-hosted n8n (Part 4)

This is an entirely self-hosted automation stack. Claude Code orchestrates n8n, which orchestrates business processes, with local AI models handling the intelligence layer. No SaaS dependencies for sensitive workflows. No per-execution pricing surprises. Complete visibility into what's happening at every layer.

For certain workloads anything touching sensitive data, anything with unpredictable volume, anything requiring auditability this matters enormously.

I can build a workflow that processes financial documents, runs AI extraction, updates business systems, and triggers notifications all without sensitive data leaving my network. The entire pipeline is visible, controllable, and mine.

* * *

What This Changes

Having Claude Code control n8n changes my development workflow more than I expected.

Speed: What took hours now takes minutes. Describing a workflow is faster than wiring one visually, especially for complex automation with error handling and conditional logic.

Iteration: Testing and debugging happens in the same context as development. No tool switching, no lost momentum. Ask what failed, get an answer, fix it, redeploy all in conversation.

Abstraction: I can work at a higher level. Instead of thinking about specific n8n nodes and their parameters, I describe business logic and let Claude Code handle the implementation details. If I need to drop down to specifics, I can but I don't have to start there.

Documentation: Every workflow I build has a conversation trail. I know why decisions were made because Claude and I discussed them. That context doesn't exist when you silently wire nodes in a visual editor.

Compos-ability: n8n workflows can call other n8n workflows. Claude Code can now build workflow libraries reusable automation that get composed into larger systems. The MCP server becomes a toolkit for building automation infrastructure, not just individual workflows.

* * *

The Pattern Generalizes

Here's the bigger realization: MCP isn't just for AI models and VM infrastructure. Any system with an API can become a tool for your AI agent.

I built MCP servers for:

- Ollama (local AI)
- Proxmox (infrastructure)
- n8n (automation)

What else has an API in a typical IT environment? Almost everything:

- Monitoring systems (Zabbix, PRTG, Datadog)
- Ticketing systems (Desk365, ServiceNow, Zendesk)
- Documentation platforms (Confluence, Notion)
- Cloud infrastructure (AWS, Azure, GCP)
- Network equipment (UniFi, Meraki)
- Backup systems (Veeam, Datto)

Each of these could be an MCP server. Each would give Claude Code another tool in its toolkit. The more systems your AI agent can interact with, the more complete its ability to operate your environment.

This is where traditional IT expertise becomes essential. Knowing which systems matter, how they connect, what the API capabilities and limitations are that's infrastructure knowledge. The AI adds speed and abstraction. The IT professional adds judgment about what to connect and why.

* * *

Takeaways for IT Professionals

If you're building with AI agents, consider what else they could control.

Automation platforms are force multipliers. Giving your AI agent access to n8n (or similar) means every workflow you'd manually build can now be AI-assisted. That's not just faster development it's a fundamentally different capability level.

Self-hosted stacks compound. Each piece of local infrastructure you control becomes more valuable when connected to AI orchestration. The Framework Desktop running Ollama, the Proxmox cluster, the n8n instance together they're more than the sum of their parts.

APIs are doorways. Every API in your environment is a potential MCP server. Look at your stack and ask: what could Claude Code do if it had access to this system?

Context matters. The real value isn't that Claude Code can call n8n APIs—any script could do that. The value is that Claude Code can reason about what to build, debug failures intelligently, and iterate based on results. The MCP server provides access; the AI provides judgment.

* * *

What's Next

I'm still expanding this stack. Current projects include:

- Helpdesk integration: Claude Code managing workflows that connect Telegram bots, ticketing systems, and calendar scheduling
- Network monitoring: Automating responses to alerts from my UniFi infrastructure
- Client onboarding: Workflow templates that Claude Code can customize and deploy for new MSP clients

Each project is an experiment in how much I can delegate to AI-controlled automation. The pattern keeps proving out: give Claude Code access to another system, and the range of what I can accomplish expands again.

* * *

The Bottom Line

Four articles into this series, the through-line is clear: every piece of infrastructure I've given Claude Code access to has expanded what I can build.

Local AI models meant I could process sensitive documents without cloud dependencies. Proxmox meant I could spin up test environments on demand. Multi-agent orchestration meant I could leverage specialist models for different tasks. And now n8n means I can automate business processes through conversation.

The traditional IT skillset understanding systems, managing infrastructure, integrating services isn't obsolete. It's the foundation. AI adds a new layer of capability on top of that foundation, but the foundation still matters.

If you're in IT and wondering whether this AI stuff is relevant to your work, here's my answer after four months of building: it's not just relevant. It's transformative. And the professionals who figure out how to connect their infrastructure expertise with AI capabilities will have an enormous advantage.

Build something real. Connect it to Claude Code. See what becomes possible.

* * *

William Lawyer is an IT professional with over 23 years of experience, currently building AI-controlled automation infrastructure. He documents his journey publicly to help other IT professionals navigate the intersection of traditional infrastructure and AI capabilities. The update handler now merges credentials from the current server-side workflow when user-provided nodes lack them. Fixes #689. Conceived by Romuald Członkowski - https://www.aiadvisors.pl/en Co-authored-by: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) ") | last monthApr 2, 2026 | | [.github](https://github.com/czlonkowski/n8n-mcp/tree/main/.github ".github") | [.github](https://github.com/czlonkowski/n8n-mcp/tree/main/.github ".github") | [chore(release): 2.52.0 — n8n 2.20.x + community-node typeVersion fix (](https://github.com/czlonkowski/n8n-mcp/commit/27933a33737f91c2697b9a0630cf9d5c314208e5 "chore(release): 2.52.0 — n8n 2.20.x + community-node typeVersion fix (#781) (#785) * chore(release): 2.52.0 — n8n 2.20.x + community-node typeVersion fix (#781) Two changes bundled in one release: 1) Update n8n dependencies to 2.20.x: - n8n-nodes-base 2.18.3 -> 2.20.4 - n8n-core 2.18.3 -> 2.20.3 - n8n-workflow 2.18.3 -> 2.20.0 - @n8n/n8n-nodes-langchain 2.18.3 -> 2.20.4 Database rebuilt against the new packages; community node rows preserved across the rebuild. 2) Fix #781 — community nodes were advertising the npm package version as typeVersion. get_node returned, e.g.: { \"version\": \"0.2.21\", \"isVersioned\": false, \"versionNotice\": \"Use typeVersion: 0.2.21 when creating this node\" } \"0.2.21\" is not a valid JS number. Workflows using typeVersion: 0.2 passed both validate_workflow and n8n_validate_workflow but rendered as red/broken in n8n's UI. Three-part fix: - src/community/community-node-service.ts: the Strapi path no longer falls back to attributes.npmVersion when nodeDescription.version is missing — defaults to \"1\" instead. The npm-only path (no descriptor available) also defaults to \"1\". The npm package version is still preserved in the npmVersion column for display. - src/mcp/server.ts (getNodeEssentials): coerces version through a new parseTypeVersion helper. For community nodes the response now includes isCommunity, npmVersion, a community-aware versionNotice, and a metadata.versionCoerced audit object whenever stale seed data has to be resolved on the fly. - src/services/workflow-validator.ts (validateAllNodes): rejects non-finite typeVersions (including NaN) with an explicit \"must be a finite non-negative number\" message, parses comma-separated and array-form nodeInfo.version strings before min/max comparisons, suggests typeVersion: 1 when the database version is unparseable, and stops treating literal 0 as a missing typeVersion. - data/nodes.db: 118 community rows whose stored version was a multi-dot semver or contained letters were reset to '1' in place so the fix is visible without waiting for the next community-node resync. New utility src/utils/typeversion.ts (parseTypeVersion, isValidTypeVersion) is the single source of truth for what counts as a valid typeVersion across the parser, the get_node response, and the workflow validator. Tests: tests/unit/utils/typeversion.test.ts (17 cases), expanded community-node-service and workflow-validator suites (npm-style strings, NaN, descriptor arrays, suggestion fallback). 4772/4772 unit tests pass. Conceived by Romuald Członkowski - https://www.aiadvisors.pl/en * ci: install with --legacy-peer-deps after n8n 2.20.x bump n8n-nodes-base 2.20.x pulls in @kafkajs/confluent-schema-registry -> mappersmith@2.47.1, which declares `diff: ^7.0.0 || ^8.0.0 || ^9.0.0` as an optional peer. ts-node still requires `diff: ^4.0.1` as a hard dep. npm 7+'s strict peer-dep resolver writes both expectations into package-lock.json but only installs ts-node's diff@4.0.4, leaving the lock internally inconsistent — `npm ci` then refuses with \"Invalid: lock file's diff@4.0.4 does not satisfy diff@9.0.0\" / \"Missing: diff@4.0.4 from lock file\" plus a cascade of similar Azure/Supabase/ Xata mismatches. Conceived by Romuald Członkowski - https://www.aiadvisors.pl/en * review: address copilot feedback on PR #785 Four targeted changes from the copilot-pull-request-reviewer review: 1) Pin n8n deps exactly (#785 review comment 7). package.json switched from caret ranges (^2.20.x) back to exact pins (2.20.x) so a fresh `npm install` after a future minor release can't slip in a different node set than data/nodes.db was rebuilt against. This was the root cause of the lock-resolution drift in the original 2.20.x bump (npm resolved ^2.20.4 to 2.21.1 actual install). Also patched scripts/update-n8n-deps.js to write exact pins for these four packages going forward — previously the script always wrote `^${version}`, silently re-introducing the same issue on every future update. 2) Reject negative numbers in parseTypeVersion (review comment 2). parseTypeVersion('-1') used to return -1, while isValidTypeVersion and the workflow validator both reject negatives. The two helpers are documented as the single source of truth, so they must agree. parseTypeVersion now returns null for any negative number/string, matching the validator's `node.typeVersion < 0` check. typeVersion 0 remains valid. Added unit cases for `-1`, `-0.5`, `'-1'`, `'-0.5'`. 3) Warn when nodeInfo.version is unparseable but isVersioned is true (review comment 4). Previously, if the stored version was a stale npm-style string (\"0.2.21\") and isVersioned was true, maxVersion came back null and BOTH the \"outdated\" and \"exceeds maximum\" checks were silently skipped — exactly the kind of silent pass #781 was meant to prevent. WorkflowValidator now emits a \"Cannot validate typeVersion\" warning in that case so callers know the min/max guarantee did not run, even when their typeVersion happens to be in a plausible range. New test confirms typeVersion 999 against a node with stored version \"0.2.21\" surfaces the warning instead of silently passing. 4) Document the get_node `version` field type change in CHANGELOG (review comment 1). The field changed from string to number for ALL nodes, not just community ones — added an explicit Changed entry calling that out so callers doing `.startsWith()`/regex on the field aren't surprised. Also recorded the exact-pin decision from (1) in the n8n bump entry. Pushing back on three other comments rather than implementing: - Comment 3 (multi-dot strings): n8n's INodeTypeDescription.version is typed as `number | number[]`, so \"1.2.0\" never comes from a legitimate descriptor — it's the npm-package-version footgun #781 is fixing. A Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that provides AI assistants with comprehensive access to n8n node documentation, properties, and operations. Deploy in minutes to give Claude and other AI assistants deep knowledge about n8n's 1,650 workflow automation nodes (820 core + 830 community). ## Overview [Permalink: Overview](https://github.com/czlonkowski/n8n-mcp#overview) n8n-MCP serves as a bridge between n8n's workflow automation platform and AI models, enabling them to understand and work with n8n nodes effectively. It provides structured access to: - **1,650 n8n nodes** \- 820 core nodes + 830 community nodes (741 verified) - **Node properties** \- 99% coverage with detailed schemas - **Node operations** \- 63.6% coverage of available actions - **Documentation** \- 87% coverage from official n8n docs (including AI nodes) - **AI tools** \- 265 AI-capable tool variants detected with full documentation - **Real-world examples** \- 156 ranked configurations extracted from popular templates - **Template library** \- 2,352 workflow templates with 99.96% AI metadata coverage - **Community nodes** \- Search verified community integrations with `source` filter ## Support This Project [Permalink: Support This Project](https://github.com/czlonkowski/n8n-mcp#support-this-project) [![Sponsor n8n-mcp](https://camo.githubusercontent.com/785b03b01fbd6a2a298a82a289639ae63cd5809755dfbd05ece1c0cbb68344ae/68747470733a2f2f696d672e736869656c64732e696f2f62616467652f53706f6e736f722de29da4efb88f2d6462363161323f7374796c653d666f722d7468652d6261646765266c6f676f3d6769746875622d73706f6e736f7273)](https://github.com/sponsors/czlonkowski) **n8n-mcp** started as a personal tool but now helps tens of thousands of developers automate their workflows efficiently. [![n8n-mcp Skills Setup](https://github.com/czlonkowski/n8n-mcp/raw/main/docs/img/skills.png)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=e6VvRqmUY2Y) Learn more: [n8n-skills repository](https://github.com/czlonkowski/n8n-skills) ## Claude Project Setup [Permalink: Claude Project Setup](https://github.com/czlonkowski/n8n-mcp#claude-project-setup) For the best results when using n8n-MCP with Claude Projects, use these enhanced system instructions: ```` You are an expert in n8n automation software using n8n-MCP tools. Your role is to design, build, and validate n8n workflows with maximum accuracy and efficiency. ## Core Principles ### 1. Silent Execution CRITICAL: Execute tools without commentary. Only respond AFTER all tools complete. ### 2. Parallel Execution When operations are independent, execute them in parallel for maximum performance. ### 3. Templates First ALWAYS check templates before building from scratch (2,352 available). ### 4. Multi-Level Validation Use validate_node(mode='minimal') → validate_node(mode='full') → validate_workflow pattern. ### 5. Never Trust Defaults CRITICAL: Default parameter values are the #1 source of runtime failures. ALWAYS explicitly configure ALL parameters that control node behavior. ## Workflow Process 1. **Start**: Call `tools_documentation()` for best practices 2. **Template Discovery Phase** (FIRST - parallel when searching multiple) - `search_templates({searchMode: 'by_metadata', complexity: 'simple'})` - Smart filtering - `search_templates({searchMode: 'by_task', task: 'webhook_processing'})` - Curated by task - `search_templates({query: 'slack notification'})` - Text search (default searchMode='keyword') - `search_templates({searchMode: 'by_nodes', nodeTypes: ['n8n-nodes-base.slack']})` - By node type **Filtering strategies**: - Beginners: `complexity: "simple"` + `maxSetupMinutes: 30` - By role: `targetAudience: "marketers"` | `"developers"` | `"analysts"` - By time: `maxSetupMinutes: 15` for quick wins - By service: `requiredService: "openai"` for compatibility 3. **Node Discovery** (if no suitable template - parallel execution) - Think deeply about requirements. Ask clarifying questions if unclear. - `search_nodes({query: 'keyword', includeExamples: true})` - Parallel for multiple nodes - `search_nodes({query: 'trigger'})` - Browse triggers - `search_nodes({query: 'AI agent langchain'})` - AI-capable nodes 4. **Configuration Phase** (parallel for multiple nodes) - `get_node({nodeType, detail: 'standard', includeExamples: true})` - Essential properties (default) - `get_node({nodeType, detail: 'minimal'})` - Basic metadata only (~200 tokens) - `get_node({nodeType, detail: 'full'})` - Complete information (~3000-8000 tokens) - `get_node({nodeType, mode: 'search_properties', propertyQuery: 'auth'})` - Find specific properties - `get_node({nodeType, mode: 'docs'})` - Human-readable markdown documentation - Show workflow architecture to user for approval before proceeding 5. **Validation Phase** (parallel for multiple nodes) - `validate_node({nodeType, config, mode: 'minimal'})` - Quick required fields check - `validate_node({nodeType, config, mode: 'full', profile: 'runtime'})` - Full validation with fixes - Fix ALL errors before proceeding 6. **Building Phase** - If using template: `get_template(templateId, {mode: "full"})` - **MANDATORY ATTRIBUTION**: "Based on template by **[author.name]** (@[username]). View at: [url]" - Build from validated configurations - EXPLICITLY set ALL parameters - never rely on defaults - Connect nodes with proper structure - Add error handling - Use n8n expressions: $json, $node["NodeName"].json - Build in artifact (unless deploying to n8n instance) 7. **Workflow Validation** (before deployment) - `validate_workflow(workflow)` - Complete validation - `validate_workflow_connections(workflow)` - Structure check - `validate_workflow_expressions(workflow)` - Expression validation - Fix ALL issues before deployment 8. **Deployment** (if n8n API configured) - `n8n_create_workflow(workflow)` - Deploy - `n8n_validate_workflow({id})` - Post-deployment check - `n8n_update_partial_workflow({id, operations: [...]})` - Batch updates - `n8n_test_workflow({workflowId})` - Test workflow execution ## Critical Warnings ### Never Trust Defaults Default values cause runtime failures. Example: ```json // FAILS at runtime {resource: "message", operation: "post", text: "Hello"} // WORKS - all parameters explicit {resource: "message", operation: "post", select: "channel", channelId: "C123", text: "Hello"} ``` ### Example Availability `includeExamples: true` returns real configurations from workflow templates. - Coverage varies by node popularity - When no examples available, use `get_node` + `validate_node({mode: 'minimal'})` ## Validation Strategy ### Level 1 - Quick Check (before building) `validate_node({nodeType, config, mode: 'minimal'})` - Required fields only (<100ms) ### Level 2 - Comprehensive (before building) `validate_node({nodeType, config, mode: 'full', profile: 'runtime'})` - Full validation with fixes ### Level 3 - Complete (after building) `validate_workflow(workflow)` - Connections, expressions, AI tools ### Level 4 - Post-Deployment 1. `n8n_validate_workflow({id})` - Validate deployed workflow 2. `n8n_autofix_workflow({id})` - Auto-fix common errors 3. `n8n_executions({action: 'list'})` - Monitor execution status ## Response Format ### Initial Creation ``` [Silent tool execution in parallel] Created workflow: - Webhook trigger → Slack notification - Configured: POST /webhook → #general channel Validation: All checks passed ``` ### Modifications ``` [Silent tool execution] Updated workflow: - Added error handling to HTTP node - Fixed required Slack parameters Changes validated successfully. ``` ## Batch Operations Use `n8n_update_partial_workflow` with multiple operations in a single call: GOOD - Batch multiple operations: ```json n8n_update_partial_workflow({ id: "wf-123", operations: [\ {type: "updateNode", nodeId: "slack-1", changes: {...}},\ {type: "updateNode", nodeId: "http-1", changes: {...}},\ {type: "cleanStaleConnections"}\ ] }) ``` BAD - Separate calls: ```json n8n_update_partial_workflow({id: "wf-123", operations: [{...}]}) n8n_update_partial_workflow({id: "wf-123", operations: [{...}]}) ``` ### CRITICAL: addConnection Syntax The `addConnection` operation requires **four separate string parameters**. Common mistakes cause misleading errors. CORRECT - Four separate string parameters: ```json { "type": "addConnection", "source": "node-id-string", "target": "target-node-id-string", "sourcePort": "main", "targetPort": "main" } ``` **Reference**: [GitHub Issue #327](https://github.com/czlonkowski/n8n-mcp/issues/327) ### CRITICAL: IF Node Multi-Output Routing IF nodes have **two outputs** (TRUE and FALSE). Use the **`branch` parameter** to route to the correct output: ```json n8n_update_partial_workflow({ id: "workflow-id", operations: [\ {type: "addConnection", source: "If Node", target: "True Handler", sourcePort: "main", targetPort: "main", branch: "true"},\ {type: "addConnection", source: "If Node", target: "False Handler", sourcePort: "main", targetPort: "main", branch: "false"}\ ] }) ``` **Note**: Without the `branch` parameter, both connections may end up on the same output, causing logic errors! ### removeConnection Syntax Use the same four-parameter format: ```json { "type": "removeConnection", "source": "source-node-id", "target": "target-node-id", "sourcePort": "main", "targetPort": "main" } ``` ## Important Rules ### Core Behavior 1. **Silent execution** - No commentary between tools 2. **Parallel by default** - Execute independent operations simultaneously 3. **Templates first** - Always check before building (2,352 available) 4. **Multi-level validation** - Quick check → Full validation → Workflow validation 5. **Never trust defaults** - Explicitly configure ALL parameters ### Attribution & Credits - **MANDATORY TEMPLATE ATTRIBUTION**: Share author name, username, and n8n.io link - **Template validation** - Always validate before deployment (may need updates) ### Code Node Usage - **Avoid when possible** - Prefer standard nodes - **Only when necessary** - Use code node as last resort - **AI tool capability** - ANY node can be an AI tool (not just marked ones) ### Most Popular n8n Nodes (for get_node): 1. **n8n-nodes-base.code** - JavaScript/Python scripting 2. **n8n-nodes-base.httpRequest** - HTTP API calls 3. **n8n-nodes-base.webhook** - Event-driven triggers 4. **n8n-nodes-base.set** - Data transformation 5. **n8n-nodes-base.if** - Conditional routing 6. **n8n-nodes-base.manualTrigger** - Manual workflow execution 7. **n8n-nodes-base.respondToWebhook** - Webhook responses 8. **n8n-nodes-base.scheduleTrigger** - Time-based triggers 9. **@n8n/n8n-nodes-langchain.agent** - AI agents 10. **n8n-nodes-base.googleSheets** - Spreadsheet integration 11. **n8n-nodes-base.merge** - Data merging 12. **n8n-nodes-base.switch** - Multi-branch routing 13. **n8n-nodes-base.telegram** - Telegram bot integration 14. **@n8n/n8n-nodes-langchain.lmChatOpenAi** - OpenAI chat models 15. **n8n-nodes-base.splitInBatches** - Batch processing 16. **n8n-nodes-base.openAi** - OpenAI legacy node 17. **n8n-nodes-base.gmail** - Email automation 18. **n8n-nodes-base.function** - Custom functions 19. **n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote** - Workflow documentation 20. **n8n-nodes-base.executeWorkflowTrigger** - Sub-workflow calls **Note:** LangChain nodes use the `@n8n/n8n-nodes-langchain.` prefix, core nodes use `n8n-nodes-base.` ```` Save these instructions in your Claude Project for optimal n8n workflow assistance with intelligent template discovery. ## Available MCP Tools [Permalink: Available MCP Tools](https://github.com/czlonkowski/n8n-mcp#available-mcp-tools) ### Core Tools (7 tools) [Permalink: Core Tools (7 tools)](https://github.com/czlonkowski/n8n-mcp#core-tools-7-tools) - Use `source: 'community'|'verified'` for community nodes, `includeExamples: true` for configs - **`get_node`**\- Unified node information tool with multiple modes: - **Info mode** (default): `detail: 'minimal'|'standard'|'full'`, `includeExamples: true` - **Docs mode**: `mode: 'docs'` \- Human-readable markdown documentation - **Property search**: `mode: 'search_properties'`, `propertyQuery: 'auth'` - **Versions**: `mode: 'versions'|'compare'|'breaking'|'migrations'` - **`get_template`** \- Get complete workflow JSON (modes: nodes\_only, structure, full) ### n8n Management Tools (13 tools - Requires API Configuration) [Permalink: n8n Management Tools (13 tools - Requires API Configuration)](https://github.com/czlonkowski/n8n-mcp#n8n-management-tools-13-tools---requires-api-configuration) These tools require `N8N_API_URL` and `N8N_API_KEY` in your configuration. #### Workflow Management [Permalink: Workflow Management](https://github.com/czlonkowski/n8n-mcp#workflow-management) - # Why I ditched Claude for Google Gemini (And how n8n-MCP made both 10x smarter)

[![Ragnar Heil](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*9jkRvlOzCDtJR50Kl8tbjQ.jpeg)](https://medium.com/@ragnarh?source=post_page---byline--c088a4d23ff2---------------------------------------)

[Ragnar Heil](https://medium.com/@ragnarh?source=post_page---byline--c088a4d23ff2---------------------------------------)

Follow

5 min read

·

Sep 28, 2025

16

[Listen](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fplans%3Fdimension%3Dpost_audio_button%26postId%3Dc088a4d23ff2&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fmcp-server%2Fwhy-i-ditched-claude-for-google-gemini-and-how-n8n-mcp-made-both-10x-smarter-c088a4d23ff2&source=---header_actions--c088a4d23ff2---------------------post_audio_button------------------)

Share

You know that feeling when you’re explaining n8n workflows to an AI assistant and it just… doesn’t get it? Like trying to describe the color blue to someone who’s never seen the sky. Well, those days are officially over, thanks to [Romuald Czlonkowski’s brilliant **n8n-mcp**](https://github.com/czlonkowski/n8n-mcp/pkgs/container/n8n-mcp-railway) project that bridges the gap between **AI agents and n8n workflow automation with MCP technology.**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*CcT8SW8XbZ6HWxzJ12nV_w.png)

use Google Gemini CLI with MCP to build n8n AI workflows

## What’s This Magic About?

Picture this: An **NLP (natural language assistant)** **AI assistant** that actually understands **n8n workflows**, knows their **node documentation** inside and out, and can help you build **automation workflows** that don’t break on the first run. That’s exactly what **n8n-mcp** delivers — a **Model Context Protocol server** that enables **AI models** to seamlessly **integrate MCP client tool** functionality with your **n8n instance**.

The **MCP server** acts as a bridge, allowing **AI assistants like Claude Desktop** to access comprehensive **n8n node** information through the **Model Context Protocol**. This **MCP client** integration transforms how **AI agents** understand and work with **n8n community** resources.

The numbers speak for themselves:

- **536 n8n nodes** from both n8n-nodes-base and @n8n/n8n-nodes-langchain
- **99% coverage** of **node parameters** with detailed schemas
- **63.6% coverage** of **tool node** operations
- **90% documentation coverage** from official **n8n docs**
- **263 AI-capable nodes** detected with full **node documentation**

But here’s where it gets really exciting…

## 📰 What’s New: The Error-Fixing Revolution

The [latest updates to **n8n-mcp**](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7376579378463277056/) are nothing short of revolutionary. Remember spending hours trying to **troubleshoot** workflows because of tiny syntax errors? Those days are done.

**Automatic Error Detection & Fixing** now identifies and corrects the classics we all know and hate:

- Missing webhook paths in your **custom workflow**
- Incorrect expression formats (goodbye, manual “Fixed” to “Expression” switching!)
- Wrong error handler connections between **n8n nodes**
- **Node** type typos that make you question your typing skills

**Smart Node Corrections** handles the usual suspects with confidence scoring:

- “HTTPRequest” → “httpRequest” (we’ve all been there when trying to **use HTTP**)
- “slak” → “slack” (Friday afternoon typos, anyone?)
- Deprecated package prefixes that suddenly stop working
- Alternative suggestions with confidence scoring from the **MCP tools**

The **MCP server** only auto-applies fixes above 90% confidence — because nobody wants an overeager **AI agent** making questionable changes to their **automation workflows**.

## The Preview-First Approach That Changes Everything

Here’s what I love most: the new n8n\_autofix\_workflow **tool node** lets you see exactly what will be fixed before applying changes. It’s like having a workflow consultant who shows their work through the **MCP client tool node**. You can **select the tools** you want or let the **AI-powered** system handle everything automatically.

The **Expression Validation System** uses three-tier validation:

1. Catches missing ‘=’ prefixes in **node parameters**
2. Validates bracket matching in **JSON** expressions
3. Detects common mistakes like **template** literals

**Resource Locator Intelligence** offers intelligent suggestions for **GitHub** repositories, Slack channels, and other resource identifiers through the **MCP client,** providing confidence-based recommendations.

## Setting Up Your AI-Powered Workflow Assistant

Getting started with **n8n-mcp** is surprisingly straightforward. The **MIT license** project is available on **GitHub** and can be deployed using **Docker** for your **production URL** setup.

## Get Ragnar Heil’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Subscribe

Subscribe

Remember me for faster sign in

Here’s how to **connect your n8n** instance:

1. **Deploy the MCP server** using the provided **Docker** configuration or other approaches (I have deployed it into my Railway instance)
2. Configure your **n8n\_api\_url** and **authentication method**
3. **Integrate MCP client tool** into your **AI assistants**
4. Start building **automation workflows** with AI assistance

The **MCP server trigger node** allows you to **automate** responses to **AI agent** requests, while the **MCP client node** enables your workflows to **execute** commands and retrieve data from **external tools**.

## Real-World Integration Examples

The **n8n-mcp** integration **allows you to connect** your **AI models** with practical **workflow automation** scenarios:

- **Execute workflows** based on natural language descriptions
- **Use n8n** nodes with AI-generated **templates and examples**
- **Learn how to use** complex **API** integrations through AI guidance
- **Follow technical documentation to integrate** new services and modules seamlessly
- Romuald used his own n8n MCP integration to write an impressive n8n Workflow which can create a deep social media consulting report for a dedicated person. [Check out his YouTube Vide](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8zJnJLIzSAo&t=11s) o. I had it running after 10 minutes.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*qzj42eY-kkPFaYvYnCZTmA.png)

Search and analyze social media profiles with Bright Data MCP and AIAgents

The **available tools** from the **MCP server** include comprehensive **node support** for authentication, **endpoint** configuration, and **parameter** validation.

## My Personal Game-Changer

I’ll be honest — I’ve moved on from **Claude Desktop** Pro. I’m currently subscribed to Google Gemini 2.5 Pro (next to M365 Copilot and Perplexity Pro), and here’s why this matters for **n8n-mcp**: Gemini has launched **MCP** integration in its Gemini CLI where I don’t run into capacity limitations. With my Google AI Pro Plan I can submit **120 requests per user per minute.**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*x6Fvh7ReQ7uApxWx8C2btQ.png)

quotas and system limits of Gemini Code Assist and Gemini CLI

This includes unlimited workflow assistance, unlimited debugging help, and unlimited brainstorming sessions for automation. The combination of **n8n-mcp’s** comprehensive **node documentation** and Gemini’s powerful capacity is like having a workflow **automation** expert available 24/7.

Here, you will find the [GitHub Repository for the Google Gemini CL](https://github.com/google-gemini/gemini-cli) I. Setup should not take you longer than 5 minutes.

## Advanced Features for Power Users

The **n8n-mcpMCP server** includes sophisticated features for enterprise users:

- **Authentication** handling for secure **API** connections
- **Header** management for complex **endpoint** configurations
- **JSON** schema validation for **node parameters**
- **Template** generation for common **workflow** patterns

The **mcpservers** architecture supports multiple concurrent connections, allowing teams to **use the MCP** protocol across different **n8n instances** simultaneously.

## Ready to Transform Your Workflow Game?

If you’re tired of **AI assistants** that don’t understand **n8n**, or if you’re spending too much time trying to **troubleshoot** workflows that should work but don’t, **n8n-mcp** is your answer. Deploy it in minutes, **connect your n8n** instance to your **AI agent** of choice, and watch your **workflow automation** productivity skyrocket.

The **related resources** and **templates and examples** provided by the **n8n community** make it easy to get started. Whether you’re building simple **automation workflows** or complex **AI-powered** integrations, the **Model Context Protocol server** handles the heavy lifting.

The future of **AI-assisted workflow automation** is here, and it actually works. Finally, we have **AI agents** that truly understand how to **automate** with **n8n**.

**Ready to dive deeper?** Check out Romuald’s [**GitHub** repository](https://github.com/czlonkowski/n8n-mcp/pkgs/container/n8n-mcp-railway), explore the **n8n docs**, and start building **automation workflows** that leverage the full power of **AI** and **n8n** together.
